items.passage: Supply chain management coordinates the flow of goods, information, and finances as a product moves from raw materials to the final consumer. This coordination involves many independent firms, each performing a specialized task such as sourcing, manufacturing, or distribution. [■] Because these firms pursue their own profits, their decisions do not always align with the interests of the chain as a whole. [■] A common difficulty is the bullwhip effect, in which small shifts in consumer demand grow into large swings in orders placed upstream. [■] Retailers, fearing shortages, order extra stock; wholesalers then amplify that order; manufacturers, in turn, build excess capacity. The result is wasteful inventory, idle factories, and unstable prices throughout the system. [■] Modern firms attempt to dampen these distortions by sharing real-time sales data across the chain, so that every partner reacts to actual demand rather than to inflated orders. However, such transparency requires trust between competitors and substantial investment in shared technology, conditions that many supply chains still struggle to satisfy.
paragraphs: Supply chain management coordinates the flow of goods, information, and finances as a product moves from raw materials to the final consumer. This coordination involves many independent firms, each performing a specialized task such as sourcing, manufacturing, or distribution. Because these firms pursue their own profits, their decisions do not always align with the interests of the chain as a whole.

A common difficulty is the bullwhip effect, in which small shifts in consumer demand grow into large swings in orders placed upstream. Retailers, fearing shortages, order extra stock; wholesalers then amplify that order; manufacturers, in turn, build excess capacity. The result is wasteful inventory, idle factories, and unstable prices throughout the system.

Modern firms attempt to dampen these distortions by sharing real-time sales data across the chain, so that every partner reacts to actual demand rather than to inflated orders. However, such transparency requires trust between competitors and substantial investment in shared technology, conditions that many supply chains still struggle to satisfy.

questions.stem: The word "dampen" in paragraph 3 is closest in meaning to

options.A: reduce
options.B: moisten
options.C: delay
options.D: conceal

questions.explanation: The firms share data to lessen the demand "distortions," so "dampen" means to reduce or weaken them.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 2, what happens during the bullwhip effect?

options.A: Minor changes in buyer demand become large fluctuations in upstream orders.
options.B: Manufacturers reduce capacity to match falling retail sales.
options.C: Retailers and wholesalers agree to place identical orders.
options.D: Consumer prices remain stable despite shifting inventory.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 states that "small shifts in consumer demand grow into large swings in orders placed upstream."

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 1 about the independent firms in a supply chain?

options.A: They are usually owned by a single parent company.
options.B: They may make choices that harm the overall chain.
options.C: They rarely specialize in any particular task.
options.D: They share their profits equally among partners.

questions.explanation: Since firms "pursue their own profits" and decisions "do not always align with the interests of the chain," their choices can work against the whole.

questions.stem: Why does the author describe retailers, wholesalers, and manufacturers in paragraph 2?

options.A: To argue that manufacturers cause most supply problems
options.B: To compare the profits earned at each stage
options.C: To recommend removing wholesalers from the chain
options.D: To trace how a small demand change is magnified at each stage

questions.explanation: The sequence of retailers, wholesalers, and manufacturers illustrates how an initial order is progressively amplified upstream.

questions.stem: There are four locations [■] in the passage that indicate where the following sentence could be added. 'For example, a brief weekend sales promotion can trigger weeks of overproduction far up the chain.' Where would the sentence best fit? Select a location to add the sentence to the passage.

options.A: Slot 1 [■]
options.B: Slot 2 [■]
options.C: Slot 3 [■]
options.D: Slot 4 [■]

questions.explanation: The sentence gives a concrete example of the bullwhip effect just defined in the sentence before slot 3, and leads into the retailer-wholesaler-manufacturer chain that follows.

items.passage: Soil erosion is the gradual removal of the fertile upper layer of land by wind and water. In early agricultural societies, farmers cleared land and planted crops with little awareness of this process. [■] As fields were stripped of natural vegetation, exposed topsoil washed away during heavy rains, slowly lowering yields over generations. By the early twentieth century, intensive plowing on dry grasslands made the problem far worse. [■] Loosened soil, left bare between plantings, was lifted by strong winds into vast dust storms that buried farms and ruined harvests across entire regions. These disasters finally forced scientists and governments to treat erosion as an urgent threat. [■] Researchers then developed conservation techniques such as contour plowing, terracing, and planting cover crops to anchor the soil. Modern farms combine these methods with satellite monitoring to detect vulnerable fields before damage spreads. [■] Despite this progress, erosion still outpaces natural soil formation in many areas, and poorer regions often lack the resources to apply such protective measures.
paragraphs: Soil erosion is the gradual removal of the fertile upper layer of land by wind and water. In early agricultural societies, farmers cleared land and planted crops with little awareness of this process. As fields were stripped of natural vegetation, exposed topsoil washed away during heavy rains, slowly lowering yields over generations.

By the early twentieth century, intensive plowing on dry grasslands made the problem far worse. Loosened soil, left bare between plantings, was lifted by strong winds into vast dust storms that buried farms and ruined harvests across entire regions. These disasters finally forced scientists and governments to treat erosion as an urgent threat.

Researchers then developed conservation techniques such as contour plowing, terracing, and planting cover crops to anchor the soil. Modern farms combine these methods with satellite monitoring to detect vulnerable fields before damage spreads. Despite this progress, erosion still outpaces natural soil formation in many areas, and poorer regions often lack the resources to apply such protective measures.

questions.stem: The word "anchor" in paragraph 3 is closest in meaning to

options.A: fertilize
options.B: examine
options.C: stabilize
options.D: moisten

questions.explanation: Conservation techniques are meant to hold the soil in place against wind and water, so "anchor" means to stabilize or secure it.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 2, what made soil erosion much worse by the early twentieth century?

options.A: A sudden increase in regional rainfall
options.B: Intensive plowing on dry grasslands
options.C: The abandonment of farmland by workers
options.D: The introduction of satellite monitoring

questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 states that "intensive plowing on dry grasslands made the problem far worse."

questions.stem: According to the passage, all of the following are conservation techniques EXCEPT

options.A: draining wetlands for new fields
options.B: contour plowing
options.C: terracing
options.D: planting cover crops

questions.explanation: Paragraph 3 lists contour plowing, terracing, and cover crops; draining wetlands is never mentioned as a conservation method.

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 3 about erosion control today?

options.A: It has completely stopped soil loss in most regions.
options.B: It relies entirely on satellite technology.
options.C: It is no longer considered an urgent concern.
options.D: It remains unevenly available across different regions.

questions.explanation: The passage notes that "poorer regions often lack the resources to apply such protective measures," implying control is unevenly distributed.

questions.stem: There are four locations [■] in the passage that indicate where the following sentence could be added. 'For instance, the catastrophic Dust Bowl of the 1930s drove thousands of families from their land.' Where would the sentence best fit? Select a location to add the sentence to the passage.

options.A: Slot 1 [■]
options.B: Slot 2 [■]
options.C: Slot 3 [■]
options.D: Slot 4 [■]

questions.explanation: The sentence gives a concrete example of the dust storms and disasters described just before slot 4, illustrating why governments treated erosion as urgent.

items.passage: Public health is the organized effort of a society to prevent disease and prolong life across whole populations rather than individual patients. In the earliest cities, people had no understanding of how illness spread, and waste was simply dumped into the streets and rivers that supplied drinking water. [■] Crowded conditions and contaminated water allowed epidemics of cholera and typhoid to kill thousands within weeks. During the nineteenth century, reformers began mapping outbreaks and noticed that disease clustered around particular water sources. [■] This careful observation led them to suspect a link between dirty water and infection, even before bacteria were identified. Cities responded by building sewers and filtering public water supplies. [■] These sanitary measures dramatically reduced deaths and became the foundation of modern public health systems. Today, agencies track diseases through laboratory testing, vaccination records, and international reporting networks. [■] However, global travel now spreads new infections faster than ever, so even well-funded health systems face outbreaks that no single nation can control alone.
paragraphs: Public health is the organized effort of a society to prevent disease and prolong life across whole populations rather than individual patients. In the earliest cities, people had no understanding of how illness spread, and waste was simply dumped into the streets and rivers that supplied drinking water. Crowded conditions and contaminated water allowed epidemics of cholera and typhoid to kill thousands within weeks.

During the nineteenth century, reformers began mapping outbreaks and noticed that disease clustered around particular water sources. This careful observation led them to suspect a link between dirty water and infection, even before bacteria were identified. Cities responded by building sewers and filtering public water supplies.

These sanitary measures dramatically reduced deaths and became the foundation of modern public health systems. Today, agencies track diseases through laboratory testing, vaccination records, and international reporting networks. However, global travel now spreads new infections faster than ever, so even well-funded health systems face outbreaks that no single nation can control alone.

questions.stem: The word "prolong" in paragraph 1 is closest in meaning to

options.A: extend
options.B: measure
options.C: examine
options.D: shorten

questions.explanation: Public health aims to "prevent disease and prolong life," so "prolong" means to extend or lengthen life.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 2, what did nineteenth-century reformers notice about disease?

options.A: It spread mainly through direct personal contact.
options.B: It appeared only in the poorest neighborhoods.
options.C: It clustered around particular water sources.
options.D: It disappeared once bacteria were identified.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 states that reformers "noticed that disease clustered around particular water sources."

questions.stem: What does "This careful observation" refer to in paragraph 2?

options.A: the building of sewers
options.B: the identification of bacteria
options.C: the filtering of water supplies
options.D: the mapping of disease around water sources

questions.explanation: "This careful observation" refers back to reformers mapping outbreaks and noticing that disease clustered around particular water sources.

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 2 about the reformers' discovery?

options.A: Useful prevention was possible before the cause of disease was fully understood.
options.B: Bacteria had to be identified before any action could be taken.
options.C: Mapping outbreaks proved to be an unreliable method.
options.D: Sewers were built only after vaccines became available.

questions.explanation: Reformers acted on the water-disease link "even before bacteria were identified," implying effective prevention preceded full scientific understanding.

questions.stem: There are four locations [■] in the passage that indicate where the following sentence could be added. 'For example, John Snow traced a deadly London epidemic to a single contaminated public well.' Where would the sentence best fit? Select a location to add the sentence to the passage.

options.A: Slot 1 [■]
options.B: Slot 2 [■]
options.C: Slot 3 [■]
options.D: Slot 4 [■]

questions.explanation: The sentence gives a concrete instance of reformers linking disease to a specific water source, fitting after that observation is introduced at slot 2.

items.passage: A nilometer was a structure used in ancient Egypt to measure the height of the Nile River during its yearly flood. It typically consisted of a staircase or a deep well marked with graduated scales, allowing officials to record exactly how high the floodwaters rose. The device was often placed within temple grounds, where priests controlled access to its readings. Each summer, meltwater and rains far to the south swelled the Nile, which then spilled over its banks and deposited fertile silt across the floodplain. By reading the nilometer, officials could predict whether the coming harvest would be abundant or poor. A high flood promised rich crops, while a low one warned of famine. Because taxes were assessed on expected agricultural output, these measurements directly determined how much grain farmers owed the state. The nilometer thus linked natural cycles to political power, giving rulers a tool for both planning and control. However, the readings could be manipulated, and a priesthood that monopolized this knowledge held considerable influence over ordinary citizens.
paragraphs: A nilometer was a structure used in ancient Egypt to measure the height of the Nile River during its yearly flood. It typically consisted of a staircase or a deep well marked with graduated scales, allowing officials to record exactly how high the floodwaters rose. The device was often placed within temple grounds, where priests controlled access to its readings.

Each summer, meltwater and rains far to the south swelled the Nile, which then spilled over its banks and deposited fertile silt across the floodplain. By reading the nilometer, officials could predict whether the coming harvest would be abundant or poor. A high flood promised rich crops, while a low one warned of famine.

Because taxes were assessed on expected agricultural output, these measurements directly determined how much grain farmers owed the state. The nilometer thus linked natural cycles to political power, giving rulers a tool for both planning and control. However, the readings could be manipulated, and a priesthood that monopolized this knowledge held considerable influence over ordinary citizens.

questions.stem: The word "swelled" in paragraph 2 is closest in meaning to

options.A: warmed
options.B: polluted
options.C: narrowed
options.D: enlarged

questions.explanation: The meltwater and rains caused the Nile to grow in volume and overflow its banks, so "swelled" means enlarged.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 1, where were nilometers often located?

options.A: Within temple grounds controlled by priests
options.B: Along the borders of neighboring kingdoms
options.C: Inside the private homes of wealthy farmers
options.D: At the southern source of the Nile's floods

questions.explanation: Paragraph 1 states the device "was often placed within temple grounds, where priests controlled access to its readings."

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 2 about a low Nile flood?

options.A: It was likely to reduce the size of the harvest.
options.B: It deposited more silt than a high flood.
options.C: It occurred mainly during the winter months.
options.D: It made the nilometer readings more accurate.

questions.explanation: Since a high flood "promised rich crops" while a low one "warned of famine," a low flood implies a reduced harvest.

questions.stem: Why does the author mention that taxes were assessed on expected agricultural output in paragraph 3?

options.A: To criticize the unfairness of the Egyptian tax system
options.B: To compare Egyptian taxes with those of other empires
options.C: To explain how flood measurements gained political importance
options.D: To suggest that farmers frequently refused to pay grain

questions.explanation: Linking taxes to expected output shows why the nilometer's readings mattered politically, connecting natural cycles to state power.

questions.stem: According to the passage, all of the following are true of the nilometer EXCEPT

options.A: It could take the form of a staircase or a deep well.
options.B: It was used to control the direction of the Nile's flow.
options.C: Its readings helped officials predict the harvest.
options.D: Its measurements influenced how much grain farmers owed.

questions.explanation: The nilometer only measured flood height; the passage never claims it controlled the river's flow, while the other options are all stated.

items.passage: Chain migration is a process in which migrants follow earlier arrivals from the same origin to a specific destination, guided by personal ties rather than by impersonal labor markets. The concept reframes migration not as isolated individuals responding to wages, but as a social movement sustained by networks of kinship and friendship. Each settled migrant lowers the cost and risk of moving for those who follow, supplying information, shelter, and contacts that an outsider would lack. Over time, these networks become self-perpetuating: even when the original economic incentive fades, the accumulated social infrastructure continues to channel newcomers toward the same neighborhoods. Such clustering helps explain why migrants from one village often concentrate in a single city district abroad. This concentration, however, carries ambiguous consequences. Dense networks ease arrival and preserve cultural familiarity, yet they may also confine newcomers to narrow occupational niches and slow their integration into the wider society. Critics therefore argue that the very ties enabling migration can, over generations, reinforce the social boundaries that migrants originally hoped to cross.
paragraphs: Chain migration is a process in which migrants follow earlier arrivals from the same origin to a specific destination, guided by personal ties rather than by impersonal labor markets. The concept reframes migration not as isolated individuals responding to wages, but as a social movement sustained by networks of kinship and friendship.

Each settled migrant lowers the cost and risk of moving for those who follow, supplying information, shelter, and contacts that an outsider would lack. Over time, these networks become self-perpetuating: even when the original economic incentive fades, the accumulated social infrastructure continues to channel newcomers toward the same neighborhoods. Such clustering helps explain why migrants from one village often concentrate in a single city district abroad.

This concentration, however, carries ambiguous consequences. Dense networks ease arrival and preserve cultural familiarity, yet they may also confine newcomers to narrow occupational niches and slow their integration into the wider society. Critics therefore argue that the very ties enabling migration can, over generations, reinforce the social boundaries that migrants originally hoped to cross.

questions.stem: What is the passage mainly about?

options.A: The economic wage gaps that first drive people to emigrate
options.B: The government policies designed to limit foreign migration
options.C: How social networks shape migration and produce mixed effects
options.D: Why migrants prefer cities over rural areas when relocating

questions.explanation: The passage defines chain migration as a network-driven process and then weighs its benefits against its drawbacks, covering both shaping and consequences.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 2, what do settled migrants provide to those who follow?

options.A: Direct payment of their travel expenses
options.B: Permanent employment in established firms
options.C: Legal documents required for entry
options.D: Information, shelter, and useful contacts

questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 states that each settled migrant supplies "information, shelter, and contacts that an outsider would lack."

questions.stem: How does paragraph 3 relate to paragraph 2?

options.A: It provides statistical evidence confirming paragraph 2's claims.
options.B: It restates paragraph 2 using different historical examples.
options.C: It rejects the definition of migration given in paragraph 2.
options.D: It qualifies paragraph 2 by weighing drawbacks against the benefits.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 explains how networks aid migrants, while paragraph 3 introduces "ambiguous consequences," balancing those benefits against costs like slowed integration.

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 2 about migration networks?

options.A: They can continue operating after the initial economic motive disappears.
options.B: They collapse quickly once wages in the destination fall.
options.C: They depend mainly on government recruitment programs.
options.D: They discourage migrants from settling near one another.

questions.explanation: The passage says networks are "self-perpetuating" and keep channeling newcomers "even when the original economic incentive fades."

questions.stem: According to paragraph 3, what is one drawback of dense migrant networks?

options.A: They erase migrants' original cultural traditions.
options.B: They may limit newcomers to narrow occupational niches.
options.C: They raise the financial cost of relocating abroad.
options.D: They prevent migrants from living in the same district.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 3 states that dense networks "may also confine newcomers to narrow occupational niches and slow their integration."

items.passage: Superconductivity is a state in which certain materials conduct electricity with no resistance once they are cooled below a specific critical temperature. In an ordinary metal, moving electrons collide with vibrating atoms, losing energy as heat and limiting how efficiently current can flow. Below the critical temperature, however, this resistance vanishes entirely, and a current set in motion can persist almost indefinitely without any power source. This frictionless flow arises because the electrons pair up and move through the material in a coordinated way, no longer scattering off the atomic lattice. A second hallmark accompanies this behavior: a superconductor expels magnetic fields from its interior, a phenomenon known as the Meissner effect, which allows magnets to hover above the material. Together these properties make superconductors valuable for powerful electromagnets, sensitive detectors, and lossless power transmission. However, the extreme cooling required has long restricted their use. Researchers continue to search for materials that superconduct at higher temperatures, but a fully practical room-temperature superconductor has so far remained beyond reach.
paragraphs: Superconductivity is a state in which certain materials conduct electricity with no resistance once they are cooled below a specific critical temperature. In an ordinary metal, moving electrons collide with vibrating atoms, losing energy as heat and limiting how efficiently current can flow. Below the critical temperature, however, this resistance vanishes entirely, and a current set in motion can persist almost indefinitely without any power source.

This frictionless flow arises because the electrons pair up and move through the material in a coordinated way, no longer scattering off the atomic lattice. A second hallmark accompanies this behavior: a superconductor expels magnetic fields from its interior, a phenomenon known as the Meissner effect, which allows magnets to hover above the material.

Together these properties make superconductors valuable for powerful electromagnets, sensitive detectors, and lossless power transmission. However, the extreme cooling required has long restricted their use. Researchers continue to search for materials that superconduct at higher temperatures, but a fully practical room-temperature superconductor has so far remained beyond reach.

questions.stem: The word "vanishes" in paragraph 1 is closest in meaning to

options.A: disappears
options.B: increases
options.C: fluctuates
options.D: returns

questions.explanation: Below the critical temperature the resistance "vanishes entirely" so that current persists indefinitely, meaning it disappears.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 1, why does an ordinary metal lose energy as current flows?

options.A: Its electrons pair up and move in a coordinated way.
options.B: Its moving electrons collide with vibrating atoms.
options.C: It expels magnetic fields from its interior.
options.D: It must be cooled below a critical temperature.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 1 states that in an ordinary metal "moving electrons collide with vibrating atoms, losing energy as heat."

questions.stem: Why does the author mention the Meissner effect in paragraph 2?

options.A: To prove that superconductors generate their own heat
options.B: To explain why superconductors need no cooling
options.C: To present a second defining property of superconductors
options.D: To question whether superconductivity truly exists

questions.explanation: The Meissner effect is introduced as "a second hallmark," presenting another defining property alongside zero resistance.

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 3 about current superconductors?

options.A: Their need for extreme cooling limits their everyday use.
options.B: They already operate easily at room temperature.
options.C: They are no longer useful for building electromagnets.
options.D: They lose their resistance only above the critical temperature.

questions.explanation: The passage notes that "the extreme cooling required has long restricted their use," implying this requirement limits practical application.

questions.stem: According to the passage, all of the following are true of superconductors EXCEPT

options.A: They can carry a current with no power source.
options.B: They naturally superconduct at room temperature.
options.C: They expel magnetic fields from their interior.
options.D: They conduct electricity without resistance when cold.

questions.explanation: The passage says a practical room-temperature superconductor "has so far remained beyond reach," while the other statements are all supported.

items.passage: Pollination is the transfer of pollen from the male part of a flower to the female part, enabling plants to produce seeds and fruit. Many flowering plants cannot move, so they depend on external agents such as insects, birds, or wind to carry pollen between blossoms. To attract animal pollinators, plants have evolved bright petals, sweet nectar, and distinctive scents that signal a reward. In exchange for this food, the visiting animal unknowingly picks up pollen and deposits it on the next flower it visits. This mutual arrangement benefits both partners and is a classic example of coevolution, in which two species shape each other's traits over long spans of time. Some relationships have grown so specialized that a particular flower can be pollinated by only a single species of insect. Such tight partnerships increase efficiency but also create risk. However, if one partner declines, the other may have no alternative and can collapse as well, which makes highly specialized pollination systems especially vulnerable to environmental change.
paragraphs: Pollination is the transfer of pollen from the male part of a flower to the female part, enabling plants to produce seeds and fruit. Many flowering plants cannot move, so they depend on external agents such as insects, birds, or wind to carry pollen between blossoms. To attract animal pollinators, plants have evolved bright petals, sweet nectar, and distinctive scents that signal a reward.

In exchange for this food, the visiting animal unknowingly picks up pollen and deposits it on the next flower it visits. This mutual arrangement benefits both partners and is a classic example of coevolution, in which two species shape each other's traits over long spans of time. Some relationships have grown so specialized that a particular flower can be pollinated by only a single species of insect.

Such tight partnerships increase efficiency but also create risk. However, if one partner declines, the other may have no alternative and can collapse as well, which makes highly specialized pollination systems especially vulnerable to environmental change.

questions.stem: The word "distinctive" in paragraph 1 is closest in meaning to

options.A: distracting
options.B: characteristic
options.C: faint
options.D: artificial

questions.explanation: The scents that "signal a reward" are recognizable markers of particular flowers, so "distinctive" means characteristic or identifying.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 1, why do many flowering plants depend on external agents?

options.A: They produce too little pollen on their own.
options.B: They cannot move to spread pollen themselves.
options.C: They lack the bright petals needed to reproduce.
options.D: They grow only in areas without any wind.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 1 states that "many flowering plants cannot move, so they depend on external agents" to carry pollen.

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 2 about coevolution?

options.A: It results from gradual mutual influence between species over time.
options.B: It occurs only between plants and wind-borne pollen.
options.C: It happens within a single generation of a species.
options.D: It harms both partners in the relationship equally.

questions.explanation: Coevolution is described as two species shaping "each other's traits over long spans of time," implying a gradual, mutual process.

questions.stem: Why does the author mention a flower pollinated by only a single insect species in paragraph 2?

options.A: To prove that wind pollination is more reliable
options.B: To suggest that most flowers attract many pollinators
options.C: To illustrate an extreme degree of specialized coevolution
options.D: To argue that such flowers produce the most seeds

questions.explanation: The single-insect example shows how specialized coevolution can become, setting up the discussion of risk that follows.

questions.stem: According to the passage, all of the following help plants attract animal pollinators EXCEPT

options.A: sweet nectar
options.B: bright petals
options.C: tall stems
options.D: distinctive scents

questions.explanation: Paragraph 1 lists bright petals, sweet nectar, and distinctive scents as attractants; tall stems are never mentioned.
